name: Nut
description: Nut, also known as Nuit, is the Egyptian goddess of the sky. She is depicted as a woman arched over the earth, her body covered in stars. Nut is the daughter of Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture) and the wife of Geb (earth). Her children include Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder. According to myth, Ra, the sun god, decreed that Nut could not give birth on any day of the year. Thoth, the god of wisdom, gambled with the moon and won five extra days, allowing Nut to give birth to her five children on these newly created days. Nut's role is crucial in Egyptian cosmology; she swallows the sun god Ra each evening, allowing him to travel through her body during the night, and then gives birth to him each morning, marking the cycle of day and night. She protects the earth from the chaos of the primordial waters. She represents the heavens, the cosmic mother, and is associated with rebirth and protection in the afterlife. Nut is often depicted on the inside of sarcophagus lids, symbolizing her protective embrace of the deceased in their journey to the afterlife. Her star-studded body represents the vastness of the cosmos and the eternal cycle of life, death, and rebirth. She is a powerful and revered figure in Egyptian mythology, embodying the sky and its mysteries.
subtitle: Goddess of the Sky and Heavens

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: Represent Nut's body and her role as the celestial vault.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Symbolizes the color of the sky and Nut's association with the heavens.

associations.name: Sky
associations.significance: Nut is the embodiment of the sky itself.

associations.name: Protection
associations.significance: She protects the earth from the chaos of the outer cosmos and protects Ra during his nightly journey.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Ra travels through Nut's body each night, being reborn each morning.

associations.name: Five
associations.significance: Associated with the five extra days Thoth won for Nut to give birth to her children.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Nut's Children
keyMyths.description: Ra forbade Nut from giving birth on any day of the year, so Thoth won five extra days from the moon, allowing Nut to give birth to her five children.
keyMyths.source: The Egyptian Book of the Dead

keyMyths.title: Nut Swallowing the Sun
keyMyths.description: Each evening, Nut swallows the sun god Ra, who travels through her body during the night and is reborn each morning.

keyMyths.title: Separation of Nut and Geb
keyMyths.description: Originally locked in an embrace, Shu, god of air, separated Nut (sky) and Geb (earth) to create space for life.

sources.author: R.T. Rundle Clark
sources.work: Myth and Symbol in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Explores the symbolism and meaning behind Egyptian myths.
sources.text: Explores the symbolism and meaning behind Egyptian myths.

sources.author: James P. Allen

sources.description: Analyzes the various creation myths of ancient Egypt.
sources.text: Analyzes the various creation myths of ancient Egypt.

sources.text: Translation and commentary on the ancient Egyptian funerary text.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, drink, and incense were made to Nut to ensure her continued protection and benevolence. These offerings were often presented at temples dedicated to her or at household shrines.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers
worshipPractices.description: Prayers were offered to Nut for protection, fertility, and safe passage through the night. She was invoked during childbirth to ensure a safe delivery, given her role as a mother goddess.

festivals.name: Feast of Nut
festivals.timing: Uncertain, likely tied to astronomical events.
festivals.description: While specific festivals dedicated solely to Nut are not well-documented, it is likely that celebrations were held in her honor, possibly coinciding with astronomical events or agricultural cycles. These festivals would have involved feasting, music, and prayers.

cultural.modernLegacy: Nut's image continues to inspire artists and writers. She remains a symbol of the night sky and maternal protection in contemporary culture, appearing in literature, art, and spiritual practices. Her story is used to explore themes of creation, rebirth, and the cyclical nature of time.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the beginning, according to Egyptian cosmology, there was only Nun, the primordial waters of chaos. From Nun emerged Atum, the self-created deity. Atum then created Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture), who in turn gave birth to Geb (earth) and Nut (sky). Initially, Geb and Nut were locked in an eternal embrace, preventing any light from reaching the world. Atum commanded Shu to separate them, pushing Nut upwards to form the sky while Geb remained below as the earth. This separation allowed for the creation of day and night. However, Ra, the sun god, was angered by Nut's union with Geb and decreed that she could not give birth on any day of the year. Thoth, the god of wisdom, intervened and won five extra days from the moon, allowing Nut to give birth to her five children: Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most significant myths involving Nut is her role in the cycle of the sun. Each evening, Ra, the sun god, would enter Nut's mouth, journeying through her body during the night, and be reborn from her womb each morning. This symbolized the daily cycle of death and rebirth. Another important myth concerns Nut's children. After Ra decreed that Nut could not give birth on any day of the year, Thoth gambled with the moon for extra light and won five extra days. On these days, Nut gave birth to Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder. These deities played crucial roles in Egyptian mythology and were central to the Egyptian pantheon. The myth highlights Nut's role as the mother of these important gods and goddesses and her connection to the creation and order of the world.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While Nut did not have numerous dedicated temples, she was a significant figure in Egyptian religion and was often depicted in tombs and temples. Her image was frequently painted on the ceilings of tombs to protect the deceased and ensure their rebirth into the afterlife. She was also associated with coffins, often depicted on the inside lid, providing protection and symbolizing the deceased's return to the womb for rebirth. Offerings of food and drink were made to Nut to ensure her continued protection and benevolence. Prayers were offered to her for protection, fertility, and safe passage through the night. Her role as a mother goddess made her particularly important to women, who would invoke her during childbirth to ensure a safe delivery. Her presence in funerary texts and art underscores her vital role in the Egyptian understanding of death and the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Nut's legacy extends beyond ancient Egypt. Her image and mythology have inspired artists, writers, and thinkers throughout history. She remains a powerful symbol of the night sky, maternal protection, and the cyclical nature of time. In modern paganism and spirituality, Nut is often invoked as a goddess of the night, the stars, and the cosmos. Her story continues to be retold and reinterpreted in contemporary literature, art, and film, demonstrating her enduring appeal and relevance. Her depiction as a celestial being arched over the earth has become an iconic image, representing the vastness and mystery of the universe. Nut's influence can also be seen in modern astronomical and cosmological concepts, where the vastness of space is often described in terms reminiscent of her mythological role.

shortDescription: Nut is the Egyptian sky goddess, depicted as a woman arched over the earth, her body adorned with stars. She swallows the sun each night, giving birth to it each morning.
